title: Why Porto keeps its old bridge
summary: An iron bridge from 1886 still carries cars, pedestrians, and a metro line across the Douro river in Porto. Why does the city refuse, every twenty years, to replace it?
body:
In the city of Porto in northern Portugal, there is a black iron bridge that crosses the Douro river. It is called the Dom Luís I Bridge, and it has stood since 1886. Most visitors photograph it from the riverbank. Few people ever ask why a city in 2025 still uses a bridge that is older than every car, every train, and every person alive.

The answer is partly engineering. The bridge was designed by a student of Gustave Eiffel — the same Eiffel who built the famous tower in Paris. Its iron arches were calculated for far more weight than they actually carry. When the city added a metro line across the upper deck in 2003, the engineers checked the old structure carefully. They found that the bridge was still well within its limits.

But the deeper answer is not about engineering. It is about decisions. Many cities, when their old bridges grow tired, choose to replace them. A new bridge is often cheaper to build than to maintain an old one for another century. The economics are clear. Porto chose differently.

A retired engineer named Dona Rosa, who worked on the metro project, once explained the choice to a journalist. 'Every twenty years,' she said, 'someone proposes that we replace it. Every twenty years, we say no. The reason is not nostalgia. The reason is that this bridge tells the city something about itself.'

What does a bridge tell a city? Perhaps three things. First, it tells a city that it does not start from zero every generation. Second, it tells a city that some choices made a hundred years ago are still good — and that today's choices might also matter in a hundred years. Third, a bridge that has been crossed by your great-grandmother, your grandmother, and you, says: you belong to a continuing place, not only a current one.

There are practical objections. The Dom Luís I Bridge needs constant care. The iron must be checked for rust. The metro tracks above must be inspected twice a year. The bridge is, in some narrow sense, expensive. But the city has decided that this expense is not a problem to be solved. It is the cost of keeping a thread unbroken.

In an age that often celebrates speed, replacement, and the new, this is a quieter argument. Some things, kept carefully, are worth keeping. The bridge in Porto is one of them. So is the practice of asking why we still keep it.

annotations.meaningVi: vòm sắt (kết cấu cong chịu lực)
kỹ thuật (ngành / công việc kỹ sư)
cảm xúc hoài niệm / nỗi nhớ quá khứ
ý kiến phản đối / lý do phản bác
một sợi dây liền mạch / sự kế thừa không đứt đoạn

grammarNotes.title: Hedging: 'perhaps', 'might', 'in some sense', 'partly'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Ở B1, văn nghị luận thường giảm nhẹ khẳng định bằng các từ 'hedging' để chừa chỗ cho ý khác. 'Perhaps three things.' (có lẽ) 'today's choices might also matter in a hundred years' (có thể) 'in some narrow sense, expensive' (theo một nghĩa hẹp) 'The answer is partly engineering' (một phần là vì kỹ thuật). Các từ này giúp tác giả nghe khiêm tốn và mở, thay vì áp đặt.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 argumentative writing softens claims with hedges that leave room for other views. 'Perhaps three things.' 'Today's choices might also matter in a hundred years.' 'In some narrow sense, expensive.' 'The answer is partly engineering.' Hedging makes a writer sound thoughtful and open instead of dogmatic — useful any time you state an opinion.

translationVi:
Ở thành phố Porto miền bắc Bồ Đào Nha, có một cây cầu sắt màu đen bắc qua sông Douro. Cây cầu tên là Dom Luís I, đã đứng vững từ năm 1886. Phần lớn khách du lịch chụp ảnh nó từ bờ sông. Ít người hỏi vì sao một thành phố năm 2025 vẫn dùng một cây cầu già hơn mọi chiếc xe, mọi đoàn tàu, mọi con người còn sống.

Một phần câu trả lời là kỹ thuật. Cầu do một học trò của Gustave Eiffel thiết kế — cùng người Eiffel xây tháp Eiffel ở Paris. Các vòm sắt được tính cho tải trọng lớn hơn nhiều so với thực tế nó phải gánh. Khi thành phố lắp tuyến metro trên tầng trên năm 2003, các kỹ sư kiểm tra kỹ kết cấu cũ. Họ thấy cầu vẫn còn nằm trong giới hạn an toàn.

Nhưng câu trả lời sâu hơn không phải về kỹ thuật. Nó là về lựa chọn. Nhiều thành phố, khi cầu cũ xuống cấp, chọn thay mới. Xây cầu mới thường rẻ hơn so với việc duy tu cầu cũ thêm một thế kỷ nữa. Kinh tế rõ ràng. Porto chọn khác.

Một nữ kỹ sư đã về hưu tên Dona Rosa, từng làm dự án metro, có lần kể với một nhà báo. 'Cứ hai mươi năm,' bà nói, 'có người đề xuất thay cầu. Cứ hai mươi năm, chúng tôi nói không. Lý do không phải hoài niệm. Lý do là cây cầu này đang nói cho thành phố nghe điều gì đó về chính nó.'

Một cây cầu nói gì với một thành phố? Có lẽ ba điều. Thứ nhất, nó cho thành phố biết rằng nó không bắt đầu từ con số 0 mỗi thế hệ. Thứ hai, một số lựa chọn cách đây trăm năm vẫn còn tốt — và lựa chọn hôm nay cũng có thể quan trọng trong một trăm năm tới. Thứ ba, một cây cầu mà cụ bà, bà ngoại và bạn cùng đi qua, nó nói: bạn thuộc về một nơi tiếp nối, không chỉ là một nơi đương thời.

Dĩ nhiên có phản đối thực tế. Cầu Dom Luís I cần chăm sóc liên tục. Sắt phải kiểm tra rỉ. Đường metro phía trên phải kiểm tra hai lần một năm. Theo một nghĩa hẹp, cầu tốn kém. Nhưng thành phố đã quyết định: khoản chi này không phải vấn đề cần giải. Đó là cái giá để giữ một sợi dây không đứt.

Trong một thời đại đề cao tốc độ, thay mới và sự hiện đại, đây là một lập luận lặng lẽ. Một số thứ, được gìn giữ cẩn thận, đáng để giữ. Cây cầu ở Porto là một. Việc tiếp tục hỏi vì sao chúng ta vẫn giữ nó cũng là một.

questions.prompt: When was the Dom Luís I Bridge built?
questions.explanation: 'It is called the Dom Luís I Bridge, and it has stood since 1886.'
'The bridge was designed by a student of Gustave Eiffel.'

questions.prompt: What did the engineers find when they checked the bridge in 2003?
options: The bridge was failing and would need to be replaced.
The bridge was still well within its weight limits.
The bridge was wrong for trains.
questions.explanation: 'They found that the bridge was still well within its limits.'
questions.hint: Paragraph 2, after the metro is added.

questions.prompt: Why do many cities choose to replace tired bridges?
options: It is often cheaper than maintaining the old one for another century.
Engineers refuse to fix old bridges.
questions.explanation: 'A new bridge is often cheaper to build than to maintain an old one for another century.'

questions.prompt: How often is the question of replacement raised in Porto, according to Dona Rosa?
questions.explanation: 'Every twenty years, someone proposes that we replace it. Every twenty years, we say no.'

questions.prompt: According to Dona Rosa, what is NOT the reason for keeping the bridge?
questions.hint: Look at Dona Rosa's exact words about the reason.

questions.prompt: What three things does the writer suggest a bridge tells a city?
options: That it doesn't start from zero each generation; that some old choices are still good; that you belong to a continuing place
That cars are bad; that water is dangerous; that engineers are heroes
That tourists are good for the economy; that history is irrelevant; that taxes should be raised
That bridges are art; that politicians are wrong; that the new is always better
questions.explanation: The three numbered items in paragraph 5.

questions.prompt: What practical objection does the writer admit?
options: The bridge needs constant care: rust checks, twice-yearly inspections — it is, in a narrow sense, expensive.
The Douro river is too deep.

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe the cost of maintenance?
options: A problem to be solved as quickly as possible
Not a problem to be solved — it is the cost of keeping a thread unbroken
An ordinary bill like any other
questions.explanation: 'But the city has decided that this expense is not a problem to be solved. It is the cost of keeping a thread unbroken.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the essay NOT claiming?
options: All old bridges everywhere must be preserved at any cost.
Some old structures, carefully kept, are worth their maintenance cost.
The reason to keep the bridge is not nostalgia alone.
The practice of asking why we keep something is itself valuable.
questions.explanation: The writer never argues for blanket preservation — only for thoughtful preservation, and uses hedges throughout.

questions.prompt: Why is the essay's argument called 'a quieter argument'?
options: It is written in a small font.
It runs against the louder cultural pressure to celebrate speed, replacement, and the new.
questions.explanation: 'In an age that often celebrates speed, replacement, and the new, this is a quieter argument.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the essay?
options: Keeping an old structure can be a meaningful, expensive, and deliberate civic choice — not just sentimentality.
Iron bridges are the strongest type.
Porto is the most beautiful city in Portugal.
questions.explanation: The essay frames the bridge as a chosen, defended decision, not an accident of inertia.
questions.hint: Think about the difference between 'kept by accident' and 'kept by decision.'

cloze.template:
In the city of Porto in northern Portugal, there is a black iron bridge that crosses the Douro river. It is called the Dom Luís I Bridge, and it has stood since {{b1}}. Most visitors photograph it from the riverbank. Few people ever ask why a city in 2025 still uses a bridge that is older than every car, every train, and every person alive.

The answer is partly {{b2}}. The bridge was designed by a student of Gustave Eiffel — the same Eiffel who built the famous tower in Paris. They found that the bridge was still well within its {{b3}}.

But the deeper answer is not about engineering. It is about {{b4}}. Many cities, when their old bridges grow tired, choose to replace them. A new bridge is often cheaper to build than to maintain an old one for another century. The economics are clear. Porto chose differently.

A retired engineer named Dona Rosa, who worked on the metro project, once explained the choice to a journalist. 'Every twenty years,' she said, 'someone proposes that we replace it. Every twenty years, we say {{b5}}. The reason is not nostalgia. The reason is that this bridge tells the city something about itself.'

What does a bridge tell a city? Perhaps three things. First, it tells a city that it does not start from {{b6}} every generation. Second, it tells a city that some choices made a hundred years ago are still good — and that today's choices {{b7}} also matter in a hundred years. Third, a bridge that has been crossed by your great-grandmother, your grandmother, and you, says: you belong to a continuing place, not only a current one.

There are practical objections. The Dom Luís I Bridge needs constant {{b8}}. The iron must be checked for rust. The metro tracks above must be inspected twice a year. The bridge is, in some narrow {{b9}}, expensive. But the city has decided that this expense is not a problem to be solved. It is the cost of keeping a thread {{b10}}.

In an age that often celebrates speed, replacement, and the new, this is a quieter argument. Some things, kept carefully, are worth keeping. The bridge in Porto is one of them. So is the practice of asking why we still keep {{b11}}.

blanks.explanation: The deeper answer is about decisions.
Every twenty years, they say no.
It does not start from zero each generation.
Hedged with 'might' — today's choices might matter.
The cost of keeping a thread unbroken.
Why we still keep it (the bridge).

criticalThinkingQuestion: Dona Rosa says the bridge is not kept out of nostalgia but because 'it tells the city something about itself.' Is there an old object, building, or routine in your own life that you keep for a similar reason — not because you miss the past, but because the thing still tells you something? What would change if you let it go?
